planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Cootamundra wattle is fast-growing and may bloom in 2–3 years from seed. It is short-lived (10–15 years) but spectacular during its prime years. In zone 9 it can be grown as a large shrub or small tree reaching 15–20 feet. Seed coats require nicking or brief boiling water treatment before sowing. In southern and eastern Australia it is listed as invasive outside its native range. All plant parts are potential allergens.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Cootamundra wattle is one of the most widely grown and most spectacular of the Australian wattles — a fast-growing, relatively short-lived (10–15 year) small tree or large shrub of exceptional beauty in late winter and early spring when the entire tree is covered in a cloud of hundreds of tiny, intensely fragrant, bright golden-yellow, spherical flower pom-poms against a foil of finely divided, beautiful, silver-blue to blue-gray, twice-pinnate foliage. The silver-blue leaf color and golden-yellow flowers in combination create a vivid contrast of cool silver and warm gold that is one of the most striking color combinations in all of winter horticulture, and a tree in full bloom — the silvery foliage invisible beneath a total coating of brilliant, fragrant, golden-yellow — represents one of the most theatrical winter spectacles in zone 9 gardens. The flowers are intensely fragrant, the sweet, honey-like scent carrying for considerable distances in calm winter air. Fast-growing, drought-tolerant once established, and relatively inexpensive to purchase — making it one of the most rewarding, high-impact winter-flowering investments in the zone 9 garden. Short-lived but worth replanting.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Queensland silver wattle is a phyllode-leaved acacia (unlike A. baileyana which has bipinnate leaves) with large, oval, silver-gray phyllodes (flattened leaf stalks that function as leaves). It blooms in winter–spring, producing masses of fragrant, golden-yellow flower pom-poms. Fast-growing to 15–25 feet. Seed requires hot-water scarification. Notable for its silver-metallic foliage effect year-round.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Queensland silver wattle provides two distinct ornamental qualities in one fast-growing tree: outstanding silver foliage year-round and spectacular, fragrant winter-spring flowers. The large, oval, silvery-gray to blue-silver phyllodes (the leaf-like flattened leaf stalks that the phyllode-bearing acacias use in place of true leaves) create a remarkable, shining, metallic-silver to pewter-gray foliage effect that is one of the most silver of any large tree — the whole tree appearing as if molded from silver-gray metal. Against this striking silver backdrop, winter through spring brings dense, spherical, golden-yellow flower pompoms in long, arching racemes that arch along every stem — the most vivid, warm golden-yellow contrasting brilliantly with the cool silver-gray phyllodes. The flowers are sweetly fragrant. Native to the dry subtropical forests and scrubs of Queensland and New South Wales, it is well-adapted to dry, warm conditions and thrives in zone 9–11 gardens with minimal supplemental irrigation once established. One of the most visually striking large trees for the warm-climate garden, offering both a dramatic, year-round silver landscape presence and a spectacular winter flower display.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver wattle is the primary source of 'mimosa' cut flowers that have been emblematic of late winter in the south of France and the Italian Riviera since the 19th century. It blooms in January–March (late winter), producing masses of fragrant, golden-yellow flower pom-poms. More cold-hardy than some acacias (to zone 8a). Fast-growing; potentially invasive in some Mediterranean climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; silver wattle is the 'mimosa' of European florists and the flower market — the tree that has been synonymous with late winter in southern France and the Italian Riviera since the mid-19th century when Australian plant introductions transformed the Côte d'Azur landscape. Each February the entire region bursts into the golden-yellow bloom of silver wattle, and 'mimosa' trains and festivals celebrate the spectacle; the cut branches of golden pompoms exported to northern Europe each winter have been a staple of the flower market for 150 years. The finely divided, twice-pinnate, silver-gray to blue-green, feathery foliage creates an extraordinarily fine-textured, soft, silver cloud of foliage year-round, and in late winter the entire tree becomes covered in a total coating of hundreds of tiny, spherical, golden-yellow pompoms — each individual head scarcely 0.25 inch across but produced in such astronomical numbers that the tree literally turns from silver to gold. The fragrance, a characteristic warm, sweet, slightly powdery honey-like scent, is the definitive scent of Mediterranean late winter. One of the defining trees of the zone 9 warm-climate Mediterranean-style garden.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; river wattle has produced numerous excellent ornamental cultivars with fine weeping habits and attractive foliage, including 'Lime Magic' (chartreuse-lime green), 'Burgundy Cascade' (purple-bronze), and 'Cousin Itt' (very compact, ground-hugging mound of fine foliage). More moisture-tolerant than most acacias. The dense, weeping, fine-foliage habit is primarily valued over the flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; river wattle is valued primarily for its graceful, weeping, architectural foliage character rather than its flowers — producing dense, cascading fountains of very narrow, slightly curved, bright-green to dark-green phyllodes (leaf-like stems) of fine, soft texture that hang in long, sweeping, pendulous masses from arching branches, creating a weeping, willow-like cascade of fine-textured, green or colored foliage. The cultivar 'Cousin Itt' has become an extremely popular ground-hugging garden curiosity: an extremely compact, low, spreading mound (only 12–18 inches tall) of the same cascading, fine-textured foliage that creates a dense, completely circular, ball-like ground mound resembling the cartoon character 'Cousin Itt' from The Addams Family. Other cultivars include 'Lime Magic' (bright chartreuse-lime-yellow foliage) and 'Burgundy Cascade' (burgundy-purple-tinted foliage). The spring flowers are small, yellow, fragrant pompoms in clusters — pleasant but not the primary ornamental quality. More moisture-tolerant than most acacias, native to the stream banks and river margins of southeastern Australia.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Ovens wattle is one of the most cold-hardy ornamental acacias — surviving to zone 7b when established in a sheltered, well-drained position. The highly distinctive, angular, triangular phyllodes (the leaf-like stems) arranged in a compressed zigzag along arching stems give it an unusual structural appearance unlike other acacias. It blooms in spring, producing masses of fragrant, golden-yellow pompom flowers along the arching stems.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Ovens wattle is a compact to medium Australian wattle from the high-altitude mountain forests of the Victorian Alps and Snowy Mountains — the most cold-hardy ornamental acacia commonly available, surviving temperatures to about 5°F (-15°C) in sheltered, well-drained positions. The foliage is distinctive and easily recognized: the phyllodes (leaf-like stems) are very small, almost triangular to rhomboidal, with a sharp point, and arranged in a compressed, angled zigzag along the arching, pendulous stems — creating a unique angular, scale-like texture quite different from the fine-pinnate leaves of A. baileyana or the large oval phyllodes of A. podalyriifolia. The angular phyllodes give the whole plant an unusual structural character. In late winter and spring, the arching stems are densely packed with small, spherical, golden-yellow, fragrant pompom flowers — in some springs the stems become almost entirely golden with flower clusters. The weeping, arching habit with angular foliage and golden flower cascades makes it an excellent specimen plant for cold-climate gardeners who cannot grow the more tender Australian wattles.
